skill.name: sql-optimization-patterns
skill.description: Guides SQL query tuning with EXPLAIN analysis, indexing strategies, query rewrites, pagination fixes, batch operations, and database maintenance patterns.
skill.summary: Optimize slow SQL queries with practical indexing, EXPLAIN, and query rewrite guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell backticks, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance, but review found Markdown SQL examples and database terminology rather than executable malware. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, credential access, or executable scripts were found in the reviewed files. The remaining concern is low risk: examples include operational SQL such as COPY from /tmp and maintenance commands that users should review before running on production databases.

low_findings.title: Database Filesystem Read Example Requires Review
low_findings.description: The playbook shows a PostgreSQL COPY command that reads /tmp/users.csv. This is documentation, not executable skill code, but running it against a real database could read server-side files and require elevated database privileges.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem path is explicit and appears in an SQL example. Risk is limited because the skill does not execute the command itself.

low_findings.title: Operational SQL Examples Can Affect Production Databases
low_findings.description: The playbook includes SQL examples for creating indexes, refreshing materialized views, setting planner behavior, vacuuming, and reindexing. These are legitimate optimization patterns, but they can lock tables, change query plans, or alter database state if copied into production without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain SQL statements that modify database objects or settings. They are normal educational examples, so this is an operational caution rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Shell, Crypto, and Reconnaissance Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported shell backtick findings are Markdown fenced SQL blocks and resource references. The weak cryptography matches are SQL terms such as hash indexes or hash joins, and the reconnaissance matches are PostgreSQL monitoring catalog queries used for query tuning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are Markdown documentation and SQL snippets, not executable Ruby or shell code. The semantic context confirms database optimization content rather than malware behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Server-Side File Import SQL Example
dangerous_patterns.description: COPY users FROM /tmp/users.csv demonstrates a database server reading a filesystem path. Users should replace the sample path and verify database privileges before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path and COPY command are present on the cited line. The risk is contextual because the file is instructional Markdown.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database Maintenance Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: VACUUM FULL and REINDEX examples can lock or alter database objects. They are valid optimization commands but should be tested outside peak production traffic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly show maintenance SQL commands. They are not malicious, but they can have operational impact.

content.user_title: Optimize SQL Queries with Proven Patterns
content.value_statement: Slow SQL queries increase latency, database load, and infrastructure cost. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical analysis, indexing, and rewrite patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to read EXPLAIN and EXPLAIN ANALYZE output.
Recommends index patterns for filters, joins, sorting, JSONB, and full-text search.
Shows query rewrites for N+1 queries, pagination, aggregation, and correlated subqueries.
Provides batching patterns for inserts and updates.
Covers materialized views, partitioning, query hints, and maintenance commands.
Lists monitoring queries for slow queries, missing indexes, and unused indexes.
limitations: It does not connect to databases or run queries by itself.
Examples must be adapted to each schema, workload, and database engine.
It focuses mainly on PostgreSQL patterns with some MySQL guidance.
Production changes still need testing, rollback planning, and database administrator review.

use_cases.title: Debug a Slow Endpoint
use_cases.description: Analyze a slow SQL query, identify full scans or poor joins, and propose safer rewrites or indexes.

use_cases.title: Plan Indexes for a New Feature
use_cases.description: Design indexes that match expected filters, joins, sort order, and write volume before launch.

use_cases.title: Review Database Health Reports
use_cases.description: Interpret slow query logs, unused indexes, and table scan metrics to prioritize tuning work.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Slow Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SQL query and explain why it may be slow. Identify missing indexes, expensive joins, and simple rewrite options.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Index Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Given this table schema and query pattern, compare possible indexes. Explain the best choice, write impact, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze an EXPLAIN Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this EXPLAIN ANALYZE output. Summarize the main bottleneck, why the planner chose this path, and what to test next.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Production Tuning Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production-safe SQL optimization plan for these slow queries. Include measurements, candidate indexes, migration risks, rollback steps, and validation queries.

output_examples.input: A user lookup query filters by lowercased email and scans the users table.
output_examples.output: The likely bottleneck is the function call in the filter. The output recommends a functional index or stored normalized email column, then verifies with an execution plan.

output_examples.input: A dashboard uses large OFFSET pagination on a high-volume orders table.
output_examples.output: The response explains why deep offsets become expensive and proposes cursor pagination with a composite sort index.

output_examples.input: A report counts orders and joins users for every row in a loop.
output_examples.output: The output identifies an N+1 pattern and suggests a join or batch load approach with grouping after one database round trip.

best_practices: Start with measured slow queries and execution plans, not guesses.
Test index changes on realistic data before production rollout.
Balance read speed against write cost, storage, and maintenance overhead.
anti_patterns: Adding indexes without checking selectivity, workload, and write volume.
Copying maintenance commands directly into production sessions.
Optimizing isolated queries without measuring end-to-end application impact.

faq.question: Does this skill run SQL commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples only. You decide what to run in your database environment.

faq.question: Which databases does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on PostgreSQL concepts and includes patterns that also apply to MySQL and other relational databases.

faq.question: Can it create exact indexes for my schema?
faq.answer: It can recommend candidate indexes when you provide schemas, queries, row counts, and execution plans.

faq.question: Is EXPLAIN ANALYZE always safe?
faq.answer: EXPLAIN ANALYZE executes the query. Use caution with writes, expensive operations, and production systems.

faq.question: Will more indexes always improve performance?
faq.answer: No. Indexes speed some reads but can slow writes and increase storage. Each index needs a workload reason.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this with application code?
faq.answer: Yes. They can use the guidance to inspect queries in code and propose database-aware changes.
